channel.title: Cookings on Fernando Simões
channel.description: Recent content in Cookings on Fernando Simões

item.title: What does it mean to bind to a port?
item.description: The socket implementation is a really messy thing to do by hand. Parsing bytes and saving states of a network communication is a tremendous work, but every piece of software that is an abstraction over the network must create its own socket abstraction or use an existing one1. Sockets are so fundamental to networking communication because a network application typically consists of a pair a programs - a client program and a server program - residing in different end systems.

item.title: TCP acks and seqs
item.description: “Reliable delivery” is one of the central and most interesting subjects of the transport layer. The meaning of “reliable” was defined in RFC793: The TCP must recover from data that is damaged, lost, duplicated, or delivered out of order by the internet communication system. This is achieved by assigning a sequence number to each octet transmitted, and requiring a positive acknowledgment (ACK) from the receiving TCP. If the ACK is not received within a timeout interval, the data is retransmitted.

item.title: Testing software
item.description: I’ve been working in software development for almost 4 years, and although it’s not an extensive amount of time, I don’t think I’ve ever met a developer who liked to write tests. In fact, the tooling we have seems to confirm and endorse this sentiment. It’s hard to write tests against services running on different nodes; it’s hard to write tests against integration functionality. Mocking, faking, the London school, all these things don’t bring us glory in this ungrateful field.

A few months back I encountered an interesting behavior: I was trying to debug a legacy Flask API running on Docker. Since I had no time to setup a proper debugger, I began to add print statements to the backend code (don’t judge me, I bet you are lazy too). The problem was: the print output was inconsistent: I tried to reload the React frontend once, and nothing appeared. Then I reload again.

So many nice things happened this year. I think the last time I saw these many changes was back in 2020. I changed companies after two and half years. And I changed two times! Went from healthtech to fintech and back to heathtech. I had the chance to know great engineers in this year, and learned a lot from them. I started using vim. After one and a half year using the key bindings in VS Code, in finally made the jump.
